screens.title: Indices are shorthand for repeated multiplication
screens.body: For example, x³ means x × x × x. The index tells you how many times the base is multiplied by itself.
screens.keyPoint: When multiplying powers with the same base, add the indices.

screens.title: Use the index laws in order

steps.title: Look for matching bases
steps.body: Index laws only combine powers when their bases are the same.

screens.question: When multiplying powers with the same base, you add the indices.
screens.incorrectFeedback: For multiplication with the same base, add the indices: x² × x⁵ = x⁷.

screens.question: Choose the correct simplified form of x3 × x6x4.
screens.prompt: Use the index laws: add powers when multiplying, subtract powers when dividing.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. x³ × x⁶ = x⁹, then x⁹ ÷ x⁴ = x⁵.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. When multiplying powers with the same base, add the indices: 3 + 6 = 9. Then when dividing, subtract: 9 - 4 = 5. So the answer is x⁵.

screens.title: TMUA-style index problem
screens.question: Which of the following is equal to x3 × x6x4, for x ≠ 0?

steps.title: Start with the numerator
steps.body: The numerator is x³ × x⁶. Since the bases are the same, add the powers: 3 + 6 = 9.

steps.title: Use the division law
steps.body: When dividing powers with the same base, subtract the powers: 9 - 4 = 5.

steps.title: Choose the matching option
steps.body: The simplified expression is x⁵, which is option B.

screens.finalAnswer: The answer is x⁵ because x³ × x⁶ = x⁹, and x⁹ ÷ x⁴ = x⁵.

screens.title: You have the first index law down.
screens.body: That is one of the core tools you will use throughout algebra. Next, you will see what changes when you divide powers.

screens.title: Indices, Surds and Polynomials: key points
keyPoints: When multiplying powers with the same base, add the indices.
When dividing powers with the same base, subtract the indices.
Surds can often be simplified by taking out square factors.
Polynomials can be factorised using common factors, identities, or known roots.
reviewItems: Practise simplifying surds with large square factors.
Practise spotting factors of polynomials quickly.
